title: This Is Why Calorie Deficits Fail for Weight Loss

chunks.title: The Misunderstanding of Calories
chunks.summary: The discussion begins with a critique of the concept of calories, arguing that merely counting calories without considering food quality is misguided. It highlights how diets like Weight Watchers promote calorie restriction while ignoring the importance of nutritional value.
chunks.transcript: why are we fearing calories I think that the calories idea is kind of a scop I wouldn't be surprised if there has been some big food quote funding behind this calories idea to confuse people because this idea that you can just limit your calories without improving food quality is Insidious this is where Weight Watchers comes from and I'm I'm eating based on points or something you know and it's the idea which I think this is the this age-old kind of silly debate that we go around and around with people about on online in nutrition circles about calories in calories out and you think of course yes if you eat Twinkies in a calorie deficit you will lose weight is that the best way to lose

chunks.title: The Impact of Environmental Toxins
chunks.summary: The speaker shares personal experiences with weight loss challenges linked to environmental toxins, specifically mentioning zeranol used in cattle. This segment draws a parallel between toxins affecting cattle and those potentially impacting human metabolism.
chunks.transcript: weight necessarily I would like to think that was true but it's not always true well there will be adaptive thermogenesis and eventually you'll stop unless thyroids at zero unless you have zero zerinol in your food do you know about that oh yes yes this this mold toxin that creates well they sell the mold toxin to cow breeders so that cows get fat on 30% less calories yes so for me I actually lived in a house that has zerol didn't know it 10,000 times more estrogenic so I'm like I was starving all the time and I'm working out 90 minutes a day my thyroid is almost undetectable my testosterone is lower than my mom's in lab Des right and I can't lose weight on a low calorie diet even though well it's because of food quality in my environment yeah you know it's funny I did a podcast this morning with Dale brisbee he's a cowboy here he I think he has that show on Netflix like how to be a cow cowboy and we were talking about how to raise cattle and he was telling me about this he said you know when I'm raising cattle as a cattle rancher we give them something he didn't know it's a little wax pillow they put in the cow's ear he didn't know that it was the Zer and all and I'd heard you talk about it so I mentioned it to him and he said yeah we give this to them and we can feed them less feed and they get fatter and I thought okay Dale but you understand what's going on there right this is actually kind of a beautiful uh Insidious Black Mirror of what's happening to humans today because I think that there are similar toxins that act in similar ways in our F environment in our Ultra processed foods that are breaking our metabolism and so i' I thought that this you know when you

chunks.title: The Flaw in Caloric Assumptions
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to the flawed idea that calories can be equated directly to energy levels in the body. The speakers explain that factors like metabolic health and food quality significantly influence how energy from food is utilized.
chunks.transcript: because say you see Doritos and it says one serving is 200 calories I think about that as potential energy that's potential energy but you have to make that into physical kinetic energy in your body to repair DNA to make hormones to do all of these cellular processes and that trans ition from potential energy to kinetic energy is not assured especially if you have a broken metabolism yes by all of the toxins in our environment whether it's seed oils accumulating in your membranes causing excess oxidation or the cardial lipen in your mitochondrial chiste to be to be misfolded or you know all of these mold toxins that are affecting hormones if the transition if the actual you know movement of energy from potential energy in food to kinetic energy in your body is impaired you will simply store more of those calories so this idea that you

chunks.title: The Problem with Caloric Restriction
chunks.summary: A critical examination of caloric restriction practices highlights their failure rates and the physiological adaptations, like reduced thyroid function, that make sustained weight loss challenging. The speakers advocate for focusing on food quality rather than just calorie counts.
chunks.transcript: is completely misguided it's so false but it really is convenient for manufacturers of Coca-Cola and Doritos and Frito who can say just eat less it's funny because if you tell people to eat less calories they get Cravings of course and then they buy Doritos it's so smart there's another thing I like to share every now and then you'll see like a 25-year-old fitness instructor influencer um who's saying well I just had a diet Co to cancel out my Snickers bar and none of this stuff matters and if you just eat less calories you'll lose weight I go okay this is great uranium has a million calories per gram so if you ate that you get fat right and they go well you can't absorb it I go oh so we're asking about absorption now so you just change the rules okay and what about Zer and all if you can get fat on 30% less calories it's probably not the calories right and then they just look at me angry and then maybe they have to read a book or something well and as you hinted at if you restrict your calories also end up in this kind of this you're digging yourself in a hole right it's a race to the bottom we know we get adaptive thermogenesis as humans your thyroid hormones are less powerful or they are either downregulated at the level of conversion from T4 to T3 or T4 goes to reverse T3 or down regulated at the level of The receptors and basically your whole body thermostat gets turned down so when you restrict calories you can see the research calorie restriction fails 70 to 85% of the time for people they just don't keep the weight off so why are we even suggesting this to

chunks.title: Defining Quality Food
chunks.summary: This segment addresses the importance of food quality. The speakers discuss the criteria for what constitutes quality food, emphasizing the necessity to have open discussions about it, especially within the context of nutritionally dense versus ultra-processed foods.
chunks.transcript: look at telling someone to restrict calories as the same as when a doctor would say smoke marls they're the best it's it's just not real it doesn't work and there's no conversation or there's less of a conversation that I would hope for around food quality because I think then we have to have the hard conversations what is quality food are is meat quality food you and I believe it is but then we have to have Kans conversations with people who don't believe that you know what is quality

chunks.title: Historical Context of Healthy Eating
chunks.summary: The conversation reflects on how perceptions of healthy eating have changed over time. It contrasts past diets, which included nutrient-rich foods, with contemporary views that may not prioritize substantial nutrition.
chunks.transcript: broadly what quality food is I don't I would say any food that our grandmother recognize is step in the right direction I would say that but we live in a bubble of being abundantly healthy if you go to a stock photosite and you say show me healthy food they're going to show you a salad with 40 calories in it and some sort of unhealthy looking person eating it smiling as if they like the salad or a little piece of lean chicken when we healthy food is what the dieters plate from 1950 looked like which was a scoop of full fat cottage cheese two beef patties a piece of lettuce and a pizza of tomato I would argue about the tomato and maybe you're sensitive to dairy or not but people ate that to lose weight and it worked yeah and now the ders played is kale and quinoa
